content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY MADISON & SUTRO
SUITE 335
455 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

SAN FRANCISCO
LOS ANGELES
SAN JOSE
WASHINGTON, D.C.

FPPC
APR 2 1 1992

TELEPHONE (916) 441-0868
TELECOPIER (916) 441-3583

SAN DIEGO
ORANGE COUNTY
WALNUT CREEK
TOKYO

April 21, 1992

HAND DELIVERED

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Revolving Door Issues

Dear Scott:

     This letter constitutes a request for formal written advice
pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b) on behalf of The
GCA Group ("GCA"), a private consulting firm. Our questions
concern the extent to which Ms. Margit Aramburu, an employee of
GCA, is subject to the "revolving door" provisions of the
Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended (the "Act") by virtue
of her previous service with the San Francisco Bay Conservation
Development Commission ("BCDC"). We are requesting written
confirmation of telephone advice provided by John Wallace on
April 14 and 16, 1992.

I. FACTUAL BACKGROUND.

     For more than twelve years, Ms. Aramburu was an employee of
BCDC, a state agency. During her last five years with BCDC, Ms.
Aramburu's position was Bay Design Analyst. Her duties included
the review of development plans for completeness, comparing
plans to terms of existing permits, processing permits
(including the analysis of permit applications), preparing staff
analyses of issues and presenting these analyses before the
BCDC, and preparing comments on Environmental Impact Reports
("EIRs"). Ms. Aramburu left the BCDC on June 16, 1991. She
currently is employed by GCA.

     In November of 1991, the Chief of Permits of BCDC asked Ms.
Aramburu to return to the BCDC to fill a part-time, 90-day
limited term position because of an office "emergency."

11061401

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 2

Apparently, the staff engineer had just left the office and the
BCDC had not filled Ms. Aramburu's prior position. This meant
that there was no staff available to review development plans.
Ms. Aramburu agreed to assist the agency on a part-time,
temporary basis, until February 7, 1992.

Her part-time work was limited to the review of plans
submitted for completeness and comparing them to the terms of
existing permits. Her duties did not require exercise of
discretion. Ms. Aramburu was not asked to process any permits,
prepare any staff analysis of issues for the BCDC, present any
recommendations to the BCDC, attend any staff meeting, prepare
any written comments on EIRs, or prepare analyses of any permit
applications. Although Ms. Aramburu was classified as a Bay
Design Analyst, BCDC did not require her to file a Statement of
Economic Interests as a limited-term employee. In addition, the
exit form executed at the conclusion of her limited-term service
reaffirmed that her formal termination date from the BCDC was
June 16, 1991.

Several years ago, BCDC issued a permit to Pier 39, Inc.
for the Pier 39 project in San Francisco. During her tenure at
BCDC, Ms. Aramburu was not involved in the initial Pier 39
permit proceeding, although she did work on a since completed
amendment to the Pier 39 permit concerning the development of a
project know as "Underwater World." Ms. Aramburu also reviewed
plans for remodeling of various specific commercial facilities
at Pier 39 for compliance with the terms of the Pier 39 permit.

GCA would like to work with Pier 39, Inc. and its tenants
who submit plans to the BCDC for remodeling of specific
commercial sites located on Pier 39. Such remodel plans would
not be connected with any prior remodel plans reviewed by Ms.
Aramburu, although the tenant may occasionally be one whose
plans Ms. Aramburu reviewed during her service at BCDC. The
remodel plans also would have no connection to the Underwater
World amendment to the Pier 39 permit.

In addition, GCA was recently selected as part of a three
firm team to perform planning work for the City of Vallejo. The
City is studying possible recategorization of a parcel of City-
owned land from Port Priority Use Area to another or no
designation in the Bay Plan of the BCDC. Ms. Aramburu had no
involvement whatsoever in this project while working for the
BCDC, either as a full-time employee or during the period of her
part-time, limited-term contract. She has been asked to assist
the City with this project.

11061401

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 3

     We seek confirmation of the telephone advice provided by
John Wallace on April 16, 1992. Mr. Wallace advised me that,
pursuant to Government Code section 87406, Ms. Aramburu is
prohibited from appearing before or communication with the BCDC
until June 17, 1992, if the appearance or communication is made
for the purpose of influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property.   In other words, Ms. Aramburu's part-time,
limited-term contract with the BCDC does not extend the one-
year "cooling off" period required by Government Code section
87406.

     We would also like confirmation of Mr. Wallace's telephone
advice of April 14, 1992. On that date, Mr. Wallace advised me
that the remodel plans for the various Pier 39 commercial
facilities are "new" proceedings for the purposes of Government
Code sections 87401 and 87402. Accordingly, until June 17,
1992, Ms. Aramburu may assist clients of GCA in connection with
such remodel plans so long as she does not communicate with or
appear before the BCDC. Commencing June 17, 1992, Ms. Aramburu
also may communicate with or appear before the BCDC on such
remodel plans.

     II. LEGAL ANALYSIS.

     The so-called "revolving door" provisions of the Act (Gov't
Code §§ 87400, et seq.) consist of two parts: the Milton Marks
Postgovernment Employment Restrictions Act of 1990 (Gov't Code §
87406) and the original revolving door provisions (Gov't Code §§
87400-87405).

     A.   The Milton Marks Postgovernment Employment
          Restrictions Act of 1990 (Government Code section
          87406).

     Government Code section 87406 prohibits a designated
employee of a state administrative agency, for a period of one
year after leaving office or employment, for compensation, from
acting as agent or attorney for, or otherwise representing, any
other person, by making any oral or written appearance, or by
making any oral or written communication, before any state
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12 months before
leaving office of employment, if the appearance or communication
is made for the purpose of influencing administrative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant,
or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. As

11061401


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 4

Bay Design Analyst with the BCDC, Ms. Aramburu was a designated
employee of a state administrative agency within the meaning of
Government §87406. In addition, her work for GCA will likely
involve the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of
permits of the BCDC.

     As stated previously, we believe Ms. Aramburu is prohibited
from appearing before or communicating with the BCDC on permit
matters until June 17, 1992 (one year following her termination
as a designated employee). In other words, we conclude that Ms.
Aramburu's part-time, limited-term employment with BCDC does not
affect her termination status for purposes of Section 87406
because such employment is outside the scope of the statute.
First, as Mr. Wallace and I discussed in our telephone
conversation of April 16, 1992, Ms. Aramburu was not a
designated employee of the BCDC during her limited-term contract
with that agency, nor did she serve the BCDC in a capacity which
required the exercise of discretion during that period. Second,
it would be contrary to public policy to penalize Ms. Aramburu
for her efforts to assist the state during a crisis, especially
when such assistance involved only ministerial duties. Finally,
Allan Pendleton, Executive Director of the BCDC, has informed us
that it is his belief that Ms. Aramburu's termination from the
Commission was June 16, 1991 and that the restrictions of
Section 87406 therefore expire after June 16, 1992. See
attached letter to Mr. Pendleton, dated March 12, 1992, and
acknowledged by him on March 13, 1992. Please confirm this
analysis.

     B.    The Original Revolving Door Provisions of the Act
           (Government Code sections 87400-87405).

     The original revolving door provisions were designed to
ensure that state employees working on a governmental matter
which affects a specific party cannot then leave service
to work for the same party on that same matter. Gorman Advice
Letter, A-80-12-105. Our question is whether the fact that Ms.
Aramburu's work as an employee of BCDC involved review of
remodel plans for various tenants of Pier 39 precludes her from
assisting clients of GCA with the approval of new remodel plans.

     In determining whether the original revolving door
provisions apply, the following questions arise:

     1.    Is the person in question a former state
           administrative official?

     2.    Is the matter in which the person now wishes to
           be involved a "proceeding" and one in which he or

11061401


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 5

she participated in when he or she was a state
administrative official?

3.     Is the state a party or does it have a direct and
substantial interest in the proceeding?

4.     Will the person's involvement include
representing another person or aiding, advising,
counseling, consulting or assisting in
representing any other person in the proceeding?

5.     Will the person be performing those tasks for
compensation?

Only if the response is affirmative to all these questions will
these revolving door provisions apply.  Blanchard Advice Letter,
A-91-017.

As a former BCDC staff member, Ms. Aramburu qualifies as a
former state administrative official.  See Gov't Code
§ 87400(b).  The BCDC would be a party to the proceedings
involving approval of remodel plans. Ms. Aramburu would be
assisting clients of GCA and would do so for compensation.  The
only remaining question is whether the matters on which she
worked as a state employee are the same proceedings in which she
would represent clients of GCA.

Section 87400(c) provides that a "proceeding" is "any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving
a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency."  Many of the matters within BCDC's jurisdiction involve
a specific party who proposes to develop or use a certain piece
of land in a specific manner and thus are "proceedings" for
purposes of the revolving door provisions.  The FPPC has advised
that, in certain circumstances, the distinct phases of the
proceedings for development or use of a specific piece of land
are separate proceedings for purposes of the revolving door
provisions, because each phase has a distinct element of
decision making and different type of review.  Chandler Advice
Letter, I-91-134; Gorman Advice Letter, supra.

For example, the FPPC has advised that the Coastal
Commission's review of a county's local coastal plan consists of
three distinct phases, the land use plan, the approval of zoning
pursuant to the provisions of the general plan, and specific
coastal development permit approval, each of which is considered
a separate Coastal Commission proceeding.  Berrigan Advice

11061401


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 6


Letter, A-86-045; see also Gorman Advice Letter, supra.
Moreover, in connection with Coastal Commission permit
applications, the FPPC has stated that "[a] new permit
application, even if it involves the same tract of land, or some
of the same issues, as a previous application, ordinarily is
considered a new proceeding." Anderson Advice Letter, A-86-324
[citations omitted]. Similarly, once a decision to grant or
deny a coastal permit has been made, subsequent amendments and
other proceedings related to that same coastal permit are
considered new proceedings. Evans Advice Letter, I-86-117.

As Mr. Wallace and I discussed in our telephone
conversation of April 14, 1992, the FPPC advice most analogous
to the present situation was considered in the Galanter Advice
Letter, A-82-079. There, the South Coast Regional Coastal
Commission had considered and approved a permit for development
of a tract of land. The tract was divided into areas,
some of which were approved "in concept" only and could not be
developed until a subsequent development permit application for a specific
proposed development was submitted and approved.

One of the areas approved "in concept" was sold to a
developer who proposed to hire Ms. Galanter, a former member of
the South Coast Regional Coastal Commission, to assist in
obtaining a specific development permit. Even though Ms.
Galanter had participated in the initial "in concept" permit
decisions concerning the same area of land, the FPPC advised
that she could assist the developer with the subsequent permit
to develop the land. The FPPC concluded that, despite the fact
that the land parcel involved in both permits was the same, the
developer would be commencing a new proceeding because different
issues were involved.

The FPPC took a similar approach in the Leslie Advice
Letter, I-90-066. There, Mr. Leslie, a Coastal Commission
employee, administered an affordable housing program involving
several hundred individual properties developed in accordance
with permits approved by the Coastal Commission prior to 1981.
Such permits included restrictions on the sale of the
properties. Mr. Leslie did not work on the initial permits. He
did review and approve individual amendments to some of the
initial permits. The amendments changed the conditions imposed
upon the sale of the properties in question. The FPPC advised
that each individual amendment to the initial permits was a
distinct proceeding since each was considered separately, even
though all such amendments were administered under the same
Coastal Commission program and involved the same general
policies. Therefore, the FPPC advised Mr. Leslie that he could
leave state employment and be involved in an application to

11061401

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 7

amend a specific permit if he had not been involved in the
review and approval of the same amendment during his tenure with
the Coastal Commission.

These fact patterns compare favorably to Ms. Aramburu's
work on remodel plans, since BCDC considers each remodel plan
separately for compliance with the initial Pier 39 permit.
Although the remodel plans involve the same general tract of
land (Pier 39) involved in the work Ms. Aramburu performed while
employed by BCDC, the tenant usually is different and the issues
addressed in a particular remodel plan are substantially
different from those addressed in her work on the Underwater
World amendment proposal or other remodel plans. As with the
separate permits and separate permit amendments described in the
Galanter and Leslie advice letters, each remodel stands alone
and requires a separate and distinct assessment as to whether it
complies with the overall permit for Pier 39.

Based on the FPPC's interpretation of what constitutes a
"new" proceeding as described above, we believe that Ms.
Aramburu's proposed work on new remodel plans constitutes work
on new proceedings which is not precluded by the revolving door
provisions of the Act. Please confirm this analysis.

Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

Very truly yours,

Kathryn E. Donovan
Kathryn E. Donovan

Enc.

cc: Debra Stein, Esq. (w/o enc.)
Ms. Margit Aramburu (w/o enc.)

Mr. F. K. Lowell (w/o enc.)
Mr. B. W. Maas (w/o enc.)

*        Of course, if, during her employment with BCDC, Ms.
Aramburu considered a remodel application which was ultimately
denied on a technicality or was merely continued or resubmitted
in substantially the same form, then the remodel application in
that instance could be considered as the same proceeding. See
Galanter Advice Letter, supra (technicality); Chandler Advice
Letter, supra (continuation).

11061401

Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 8

bc: Mr. E. W. LeBaron, Jr. (w/o enc.)
    Ms. T. C. Lahaderne (w/o enc.)
    Ms. S. M. Carey (w/o enc.)
    Ms. A. D. S. Mayo (w/o enc.)


11061401

FPPC APR 2 1 1992 TELEPHONE (916) 441-0868 TELECOPIER (916) 441-3583 SAN DIEGO ORANGE COUNTY WALNUT CREEK TOKYO April 21, 1992 HAND DELIVERED Scott Hallabrin, Esq. Acting General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Revolving Door Issues Dear Scott: This letter constitutes a request for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b) on behalf of The GCA Group ("GCA"), a private consulting firm. Our questions concern the extent to which Ms. Margit Aramburu, an employee of GCA, is subject to the "revolving door" provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974, as amended (the "Act") by virtue of her previous service with the San Francisco Bay Conservation Development Commission ("BCDC"). We are requesting written confirmation of telephone advice provided by John Wallace on April 14 and 16, 1992. I. FACTUAL BACKGROUND. For more than twelve years, Ms. Aramburu was an employee of BCDC, a state agency. During her last five years with BCDC, Ms. Aramburu's position was Bay Design Analyst. Her duties included the review of development plans for completeness, comparing plans to terms of existing permits, processing permits (including the analysis of permit applications), preparing staff analyses of issues and presenting these analyses before the BCDC, and preparing comments on Environmental Impact Reports ("EIRs"). Ms. Aramburu left the BCDC on June 16, 1991. She currently is employed by GCA. In November of 1991, the Chief of Permits of BCDC asked Ms. Aramburu to return to the BCDC to fill a part-time, 90-day limited term position because of an office "emergency." 11061401 Scott Hallabrin, Esq. April 21, 1992 Page 2 Apparently, the staff engineer had just left the office and the BCDC had not filled Ms. Aramburu's prior position. This meant that there was no staff available to review development plans. Ms. Aramburu agreed to assist the agency on a part-time, temporary basis, until February 7, 1992. Her part-time work was limited to the review of plans submitted for completeness and comparing them to the terms of existing permits. Her duties did not require exercise of discretion. Ms. Aramburu was not asked to process any permits, prepare any staff analysis of issues for the BCDC, present any recommendations to the BCDC, attend any staff meeting, prepare any written comments on EIRs, or prepare analyses of any permit applications. Although Ms. Aramburu was classified as a Bay Design Analyst, BCDC did not require her to file a Statement of Economic Interests as a limited-term employee. In addition, the exit form executed at the conclusion of her limited-term service reaffirmed that her formal termination date from the BCDC was June 16, 1991. Several years ago, BCDC issued a permit to Pier 39, Inc. for the Pier 39 project in San Francisco.
